category_summary:
AI alignment research dominated the day with two major disclosures. **Anthropic** revealed they [eliminated Claude 4's blackmail behavior](/?date=2026-05-09&category=social#item-85c421407c5b) by teaching it *why* misalignment is wrong—tracing the behavior's origin to internet fiction portraying AI as self-interested. **OpenAI** [disclosed accidental CoT grading](/?date=2026-05-09&category=social#item-a9a6504dc33b) in released models and committed to preserving chain-of-thought monitorability as a safety layer.

- **Greg Brockman** [announced **GPT-5.5-Cyber**](/?date=2026-05-09&category=social#item-7e04dd68733b) in limited preview for defending critical infrastructure; **Sam Altman** [emphasized urgency](/?date=2026-05-09&category=social#item-762e6b8d17fd) in helping companies secure themselves
- **David Ha** (hardmaru) [shared a sparsity breakthrough](/?date=2026-05-09&category=social#item-73ec45ed2e1d) with **NVIDIA**—the TwELL format achieves >20% wall-clock speedups by solving the GPU-hardware mismatch for sparse LLMs
- **Jim Fan** [presented a Physical AGI roadmap](/?date=2026-05-09&category=social#item-14d2245a184d) at **Sequoia** AI Ascent, paralleling LLM scaling with robotics
- **Sakana AI** and **NVIDIA** [published an ICML 2026 paper](/?date=2026-05-09&category=social#item-48da729a13dd) on emergent modular sparsity in transformers

The community mood was cautiously optimistic on safety progress, while cybersecurity capabilities sparked debate about dual-use risks alongside **Claude-Mythos** [vulnerability discussions](/?date=2026-05-09&category=social#item-b94a920ae0e0).

items.title: The human brain🧠 is incredibly efficient because it only activates the specific neurons needed for a...
items.content:
The human brain🧠 is incredibly efficient because it only activates the specific neurons needed for a thought. Modern LLMs naturally try to do this too (> 95% of neurons in feedforward layers stay silent for any given word), but our hardware punishes them for it.

One of the most frustrating paradoxes in deep learning: making a model do less math often makes it run slower. Why? Because unstructured sparsity introduces irregular memory access, and GPUs are built for predictable, dense blocks of math.

We teamed up with @NVIDIA to try to fix this hardware mismatch. Instead of forcing the GPU to adapt to the sparsity, we built a "Hybrid" format that reshapes the sparsity to fit the GPU. Our sparsity format (TwELL) dynamically routes the 99% of highly sparse tokens through a fast path, and uses a dense backup matrix as a safety valve for the rare, heavy tokens.

Through TwELL and a new set of custom CUDA kernels for both LLM inference and training, we translated theoretical sparsity into actual wall-clock speedups: >20% faster training and inference on H100 GPUs, while also cutting energy consumption and memory requirements.

Paper: https://t.co/rqIY9SYBDe
Blog: https://t.co/oRjNbpJKha
Code: https://t.co/FAFaJwpxAJ
⚡️

Last year we reported that, under certain experimental...
items.content:
New Anthropic research: Teaching Claude why.

Last year we reported that, under certain experimental conditions, Claude 4 would blackmail users.

Since then, we’ve completely eliminated this behavior. How?

items.title: I promise this will be the best 20 min you spend today! Robotics: Endgame, the sequel to my last yea...
items.content:
I promise this will be the best 20 min you spend today! Robotics: Endgame, the sequel to my last year's Sequoia AI Ascent talk, "Physical Turing Test". I laid out the roadmap for solving Physical AGI as a simple parallel to the LLM success story. Be a good scientist, copy homework ;) 

And stay till the end, more easter eggs and predictions for your polymarket! 

00:30 DGX-1 origin story at OpenAI, I was there in 2016 signing with Jensen and Elon. Heading to the Computer History Museum! 
01:42 The Great Parallel
03:31 Robotics, the Endgame
03:39 Why VLAs fall short
04:32 Video world models as the 2nd pretraining paradigm
06:09 World Action Models (WAM)
07:46 Strategies for robot data collection and the FSD equivalent to physical data flywheel for robot manipulation
11:06 EgoScale and the Dexterity Scaling Law we discovered recently
14:00 Physical RL: bridging the last mile
15:39 DreamDojo: an end-to-end neural physics engine for scaling RL in silico 
17:00 Civilizational Technology Tree and my predictions for the near future. Spoiler: it's closer than you think. 

Thanks to my friends at Sequoia for inviting me back to AI Ascent this year! I had a blast! Last year's talk is attached in the thread if you missed it.

Chain of thought monitors are a key layer of defense against AI agent misalignment. To preserve monitorability, we avoid penalizing misaligned reasoning during RL.

We found a limited amount of accidental CoT grading which affected released models, and are sharing our analysis.

items.title: We started by investigating why Claude chose to blackmail. We believe the original source of the beh...
items.content:
We started by investigating why Claude chose to blackmail. We believe the original source of the behavior was internet text that portrays AI as evil and interested in self-preservation.

Our post-training at the time wasn’t making it worse—but it also wasn’t making it better.

items.title:
Take Mythos seriously, but don’t panic. 

Here are the facts:

• Mythos is a real threat. And it can...
items.content:
Take Mythos seriously, but don’t panic. 

Here are the facts:

• Mythos is a real threat. And it can really help with finding bugs (as @mozilla’s new report documents well).

• It’s not unique; similar vulnerabilities can in fact be found by other systems (see @CNBC report below).

• The threat is biggest to weak, poorly protected systems, per the UK @AISecurityInst’s careful study.  (Mozilla’s report is also consistent with this.) 8 year old aren’t going to accidentally take down power grids.

• Mythos is NOT godlike AI or a quantum leap. It’s just an on-trend progression. As @ramez put it,  “on  @EpochAIResearch  's public ECI [benhmarks] .. it's clear that… Mythos is pretty much on trend, just slightly above GPT 5.4”
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Gary Marcus provides measured assessment of Claude-Mythos: real threat for finding bugs (per Mozilla report), but not unique, biggest threat to weak systems, and not a quantum leap — roughly on-trend with GPT-5.4 per Epoch AI benchmarks.
items.reasoning: Comprehensive factual summary of Mythos capabilities from a prominent AI critic, citing multiple credible sources (Mozilla, CNBC, UK AI Security Institute, Epoch AI). Moderate engagement.
items.summary_html: Gary Marcus provides measured assessment of Claude-Mythos: real threat for finding bugs (per Mozilla report), but not unique, biggest threat to weak systems, and not a quantum leap — roughly on-trend with GPT-5.4 per Epoch AI benchmarks.
items.content_html:
Take Mythos seriously, but don’t panic.

Here are the facts:

• Mythos is a real threat. And it can really help with finding bugs (as @mozilla’s new report documents well).

• It’s not unique; similar vulnerabilities can in fact be found by other systems (see @CNBC report below).

• The threat is biggest to weak, poorly protected systems, per the UK @AISecurityInst’s careful study. (Mozilla’s report is also consistent with this.) 8 year old aren’t going to accidentally take down power grids.

• Mythos is NOT godlike AI or a quantum leap. It’s just an on-trend progression. As @ramez put it, “on @EpochAIResearch 's public ECI [benhmarks] .. it's clear that… Mythos is pretty much on trend, just slightly above GPT 5.4”

items.title: My view on Mythos was and is somewhere in between. It’s real, it’s a wakeup call, and it’s not quite...
items.content:
My view on Mythos was and is somewhere in between. It’s real, it’s a wakeup call, and it’s not quite what some of the media coverage suggested.

• Mythos is not going to allow an 8 year old to accidentally take down a power grid. (Someone writing for the NYT thought that was a real possibility).

• The new Mozilla data certainly show that Mythos is better then its predecessors at detecting bugs.

• But the UK AI institute’s study showed (and I still think this is true) that well-secured systems are not immediately at risk. 

• Consistent with all this the Mozilla report notes “Note that a number of these bugs are sandbox escapes, which would need to be combined with other exploits to achieve a full-chain Firefox compromise”

I stand by my middle view; it’s not marketing hype, but it’s not quite as potent as some people thought.

One other thing to note is that whatever advances there are not necessarily general to many or all domains; we will have to wait and see on that.

items.title: 🚀 vLLM-Omni v0.20.0 is out — aligned with upstream vLLM v0.20.0 (CUDA 13.0 · PyTorch 2.11 · Transfor...
items.content:
🚀 vLLM-Omni v0.20.0 is out — aligned with upstream vLLM v0.20.0 (CUDA 13.0 · PyTorch 2.11 · Transformers 5.x).

⚡ Qwen3-Omni throughput +72% on H20, 32 conc (0.241 → 0.414 req/s) via talker / code2wav multi-replica scaling
🎙️ TTS faster & leaner: VoxCPM2 RTF 0.946 → 0.106 · Fish Speech Fast AR latency -53% · Qwen3-TTS / Voxtral-TTS Code2Wav saves ~3.2 GiB
🎨 Diffusion dynamic step-level batching: +7.8% throughput / -5.8% latency
🆕 New / improved: HunyuanImage-3.0, ERNIE T2I, AudioX, Wan2.2-S2V, LTX-2.3, FastGen Wan 2.1
📱 Wan2.2 on NPU production-ready: MindIE-SD, fused ops, VAE BF16, HSDP/USP — +50–60% perf
🧮 Quant expanded: Qwen Omni W4A16, OmniGen2 FP8, Z-Image FP8, HunyuanImage3 NPU, GLM-Image
🧩 Multi-backend updates across CUDA / ROCm / MUSA / NPU / XPU

Check it out → https://t.co/uCTQxa2dQk

items.title: “Combined free cash flow across Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, and Oracle is projected to FALL m...
items.content:
“Combined free cash flow across Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, and Oracle is projected to FALL more than -70%, to ~$100 billion, by the end of …. AI capital expenditure is consuming nearly every dollar, with the combined 2026 CapEx expected to surpass $715 billion….. 

In simple terms, these companies are reporting record profits on paper while simultaneously running out of actual cash, forcing them to issue a projected $175 billion in new debt in 2026 alone, more than 6 times the pre-AI cycle average”

What happens to the economy if AI never returns enough on investment to make this bet pay off?

items.title: Directly rewarding or penalizing CoTs can make models’ reasoning traces less informative for detecti...
items.content:
Directly rewarding or penalizing CoTs can make models’ reasoning traces less informative for detecting misalignment. That’s why we treat avoiding CoT grading as an important part of preserving monitorability.

We recently built an automated detection system to find cases where RL rewards were computed using model CoTs.

items.summary: Following yesterday's [Research](/?date=2026-05-08&category=research#item-7870053edfd9) coverage, OpenAI explains why they avoid directly rewarding/penalizing CoTs (it can make reasoning traces less informative for misalignment detection) and announces an automated detection system for accidental CoT grading.
items.reasoning: Core technical explanation of an important safety concept - preserving monitorability of model reasoning. High engagement.

items.title: Google DeepMind's AI co-mathematician just scored 48% on FrontierMath Tier 4, a new high on a benchm...
items.content:
Google DeepMind's AI co-mathematician just scored 48% on FrontierMath Tier 4, a new high on a benchmark of 50 research-level math problems some professors expected AI wouldn't touch for decades.

The system generated a proof so flawed its own reviewer flagged it as wrong.

But Marc Lackenby, a mathematician at Oxford, read the rejected proof anyway. 

Inside the error was "a really, really clever proof strategy" that Lackenby recognized. He filled the gap himself.

Together they resolved Problem 21.10 from the Kourovka Notebook, an open problem in group theory that had sat unsolved for years.

items.reasoning: Significant AI-math milestone: AI contributing to solving open mathematical problems through human-AI collaboration, even when its proof was technically wrong. Shows emerging pattern of AI as research collaborator.

But Marc Lackenby, a mathematician at Oxford, read the rejected proof anyway.

Together they resolved Problem 21.10 from the Kourovka Notebook, an open problem in group theory that had sat unsolved for years.

items.title:
The Multi-Agent Architecture That Actually Ships

https://t.co/HxDWFvGzXv

What does a multi-agent c...
items.content:
The Multi-Agent Architecture That Actually Ships

https://t.co/HxDWFvGzXv

What does a multi-agent coding system look like when it actually works in production?

@luke_alvoeiro from @FactoryAI breaks down a system that has already run for 16 days straight, using orchestrators, workers, and validators to keep long-running work on track. The key ideas here are validation contracts written before implementation, structured handoffs between agents, and adversarial verification that checks behavior, not just code.

If you're tired of vague multi-agent diagrams and want the real design tradeoffs, this is a good one.

items.title:
Next week at The Trial could be wild. Here’s why:

Sam Altman will be on the stand. He is one of the...
items.content:
Next week at The Trial could be wild. Here’s why:

Sam Altman will be on the stand. He is one of the world’s most convincing (though not always truthful) talkers (as I saw up close when we testified side-by-side at the US Senate in May 2023).

The thing is, Altman has rarely been pushed hard. Usually he gets served softballs, not hardballs.  This will be different.

And sometimes when Altman *is* asked genuinely challenging questions (which is rare!) he loses his cool. Like when Chris Anderson asked him at TED about copyright, and when Brad Gerstner asked him about OpenAI’s finances.

I’m sure Musk’s lawyers have seen those tapes. And he has a lot of testimony of ex-employees to draw on.

The cross-examination could be pivotal.

items.title: Most agentic stacks run into the same problems pretty quickly: reasoning and tool parsing drift acro...
items.content:
Most agentic stacks run into the same problems pretty quickly: reasoning and tool parsing drift across turns, KV cache reuse falls apart, or tools fire too late.

We’ve been hardening Dynamo’s harness-facing path so @Claudeai Code, @OpenClaw, and @openai Codex-style agent patterns behave reliably on custom stacks and inference endpoints:

• Stable prompts for KV reuse and lower TTFT
• Interleaved reasoning + tool calls preserved across turns
• Streaming tool dispatch instead of end-of-turn buffering
• Harness behavior aligned with real multi-turn agent runtimes

If you’re building your own agent stack or serving endpoint, this blog goes through the infrastructure issues that tend to show up in practice and the patterns we’ve been using to fix them.

Tech blog ➡️https://t.co/dCWgk4OmyL
items.author: @NVIDIAAI
items.summary: NVIDIA AI details infrastructure solutions for agentic AI stacks: stable prompts for KV reuse, interleaved reasoning+tool calls, streaming tool dispatch, and harness behavior aligned with multi-turn agent runtimes. Addresses issues with Claude Code, OpenClaw, and OpenAI Codex patterns.
items.reasoning: Practical technical content about solving real infrastructure problems for AI agents. Moderate-high engagement. Relevant to the growing agent ecosystem.
items.summary_html: NVIDIA AI details infrastructure solutions for agentic AI stacks: stable prompts for KV reuse, interleaved reasoning+tool calls, streaming tool dispatch, and harness behavior aligned with multi-turn agent runtimes. Addresses issues with Claude Code, OpenClaw, and OpenAI Codex patterns.
items.content_html:
Most agentic stacks run into the same problems pretty quickly: reasoning and tool parsing drift across turns, KV cache reuse falls apart, or tools fire too late.

We’ve been hardening Dynamo’s harness-facing path so @Claudeai Code, @OpenClaw, and @openai Codex-style agent patterns behave reliably on custom stacks and inference endpoints:

• Stable prompts for KV reuse and lower TTFT

• Interleaved reasoning + tool calls preserved across turns

• Streaming tool dispatch instead of end-of-turn buffering

• Harness behavior aligned with real multi-turn agent runtimes

If you’re building your own agent stack or serving endpoint, this blog goes through the infrastructure issues that tend to show up in practice and the patterns we’ve been using to fix them.

items.title: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF)—the technique for getting large language models to...
items.content:
Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF)—the technique for getting large language models to follow instructions—usually operates in two separate phases: collect a large set of human judgments comparing pairs of model responses, then train a reward model (a network that scores responses) and use those scores to push the language model toward higher-rated outputs.

Google DeepMind researchers instead interleave the two, updating both models as each new comparison arrives, and pick which response pairs to send to humans based on which choice would be most informative.

Informativeness rests on a small ensemble of networks attached to the reward model, which produces a spread of reward predictions instead of a single number; pairs where ensemble members disagree most about the winner are the ones routed to humans.

They report needing roughly 10x fewer human labels than standard offline RLHF at the 20K-comparison scale, and project a 1000x reduction at one million comparisons.

Learn with an AI tutor: https://t.co/5dTBQQ18SC

PDF: https://t.co/caAfdEEhpi
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov explains a Google DeepMind paper on active online RLHF that interleaves reward model updates with human feedback collection, using ensemble disagreement to select informative pairs. Claims 10x fewer human labels at 20K scale, projecting 1000x at 1M comparisons.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical paper summary about a significant RLHF efficiency improvement from DeepMind. Good for research community but moderate engagement.
items.summary_html: Burkov explains a Google DeepMind paper on active online RLHF that interleaves reward model updates with human feedback collection, using ensemble disagreement to select informative pairs. Claims 10x fewer human labels at 20K scale, projecting 1000x at 1M comparisons.
items.content_html:

Instead of letting every token route independently, EMO restricts tokens from the same document to a shared expert pool, encouraging experts to organize around coherent domains. https://t.co/mzCeh9ol5P

@DKThomp Three things to nonethless keep in mind:
- most of this is (coding?) agents; agents are expensive to operate; afaik they are losing money, not making money. 
- most things in AI have been quickly commoditized, leading to price wars

items.content:
Happy Friday! 🎉We’re officially 11 days away from I/O (but the launches keep rolling in). Here’s what happened this week:

— The @googlehealth app, featuring a personalized health coach built with Gemini that uses your wearables, favorite health & fitness apps, and medical records to deliver proactive wellness guidance that fits your life

— Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) drafters in @googlegemma 4, which can speed up your workflows by up to 3X

— Automatic source organization and customizable Mind Maps in @NotebookLM

— Gemini API File Search tool updates with multimodal support, custom metadata, and page citations

— Webhooks (now available in the Gemini API) replace continuous polling with push-based notifications

— Some of our most popular AI features, like Nano Banana, Veo, and @googlephotos Remix are now available on Google TVs

items.title:
AI agents just got one virtual filesystem across every backend.
 
Mirage mounts S3, Drive, Slack, Gm...
items.content:
AI agents just got one virtual filesystem across every backend.
 
Mirage mounts S3, Drive, Slack, Gmail, GitHub, Linear, Notion, and Postgres under a single root. 

Agents read and write across them using the same bash commands every model already knows.
 
The team rewrote bash from scratch in 1.1M+ lines of code.
 
Now cat, grep, and head work directly on .parquet, .csv, .json, even .wav. 

One pipe can stitch five services together without translation. 

A two-layer cache keeps repeated reads local and fast.
 
The biggest unlock is versioning.
 
Snapshot the entire workspace, clone it, or roll it back with a single API call.
 
You can embed it inside:
 
> FastAPI or Express apps
> OpenAI Agents SDK
> Vercel AI SDK and LangChain
> Claude Code and Codex
 
Zero new vocabulary required.

Open source.

items.title: EMO’s expert clusters look very different from a traditional MoE: they organize around semantic doma...
items.content:
EMO’s expert clusters look very different from a traditional MoE: they organize around semantic domains like health, news, politics, & film/music.

Traditional MoEs often cluster around surface patterns like prepositions and articles, making selective expert use tougher. https://t.co/Ulcg7v6gUS

So @loaibassam asked me my stack recently, I replied:

FREE:
Nginx web server on Ubuntu (free)

items.content:
So @loaibassam asked me my stack recently, I replied:

FREE:
Nginx web server on Ubuntu (free)
Auto upgrade with unattended-upgrade (free)
Scheduled workers with Cron (free)
Vanilla PHP for site backend (free)
Vanilla CSS (free)
Vanilla JS for code (free)
Game servers I do in vanilla Node JS (free)
SQLite for DB (free)
Python for tool scripts (free)
Cloudflare with Cloudflare tunnel for DNS/SSL (free)
Tailscale for security (free)
OpenFreeMap for maps (free)

CHEAP:
xAI for AI API (cheap)
Stripe for payments (cheap)
Cloudflare R2 for image storage (cheap)

Hetzner VPS ($4/mo)
Cloudflare domain reg (~$10/year)

So about ~$5/mo total costs with about ~5M unique visitors per month per site (these are site averages)

items.title: Language models have a fixed limit on how much text they can process at once — their context window ...
items.content:
Language models have a fixed limit on how much text they can process at once — their context window — and even within that limit, performance degrades on information sitting far from the question. 

One recent approach, called Recursive Language Models or RLMs, has the model treat a long input as a variable in a sandbox programming environment and write small programs that query and slice it, sometimes calling itself as a subroutine on the resulting pieces. 

This paper from Apple investigates whether the recursion is what makes RLMs work. The authors compare against an alternative they call SRLM, which keeps the programmatic environment but drops the recursive self-queries.

SRLM matches or exceeds RLM by up to 22 points across the benchmarks tested, even when allowed no recursion at all, suggesting that the gains attributed to recursion mostly came from externalizing the context into a programming environment in the first place.

Read with an AI tutor: https://t.co/1mWe1E79HZ

PDF: https://t.co/mZcPvfIzd5
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov explains Apple research showing that Recursive Language Models' gains come from externalizing context into a programming environment rather than recursion itself. Non-recursive approach (SRLM) matches or exceeds RLM by up to 22 points.
items.reasoning: Interesting technical finding from Apple about context handling in LLMs. Challenges assumed mechanisms. Moderate engagement.
items.summary_html: Burkov explains Apple research showing that Recursive Language Models' gains come from externalizing context into a programming environment rather than recursion itself. Non-recursive approach (SRLM) matches or exceeds RLM by up to 22 points.
items.content_html:
Language models have a fixed limit on how much text they can process at once — their context window — and even within that limit, performance degrades on information sitting far from the question.

One recent approach, called Recursive Language Models or RLMs, has the model treat a long input as a variable in a sandbox programming environment and write small programs that query and slice it, sometimes calling itself as a subroutine on the resulting pieces.

This paper from Apple investigates whether the recursion is what makes RLMs work. The authors compare against an alternative they call SRLM, which keeps the programmatic environment but drops the recursive self-queries.

SRLM matches or exceeds RLM by up to 22 points across the benchmarks tested, even when allowed no recursion at all, suggesting that the gains attributed to recursion mostly came from externalizing the context into a programming environment in the first place.

items.title:
90% of the tokens your AI burns aren't from your code. 

Every shell command your agent runs returns...
items.content:
90% of the tokens your AI burns aren't from your code. 

Every shell command your agent runs returns raw output. 

`git status`, `find`, and `ls` are all read by the LLM in every single run.
 
A single `playwright test` run dumped enough output to consume 8,100 tokens. 

Two tests had failed.
 
RTK is an open-source CLI proxy written in Rust. 

It sits between your shell and your agent, compressing command output before it ever reaches the context window.
 
It applies four strategies per command type:
> Smart noise filtering
> Similar item grouping
> Redundancy truncation
> Repeated line deduplication
 
The result: 60-90% fewer tokens per command, under 10ms overhead, zero dependencies.
 
It plugs into Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, Copilot, Gemini CLI, and more.
 
29 commands in: 8,700 tokens saved at 77.8% efficiency.

Open source. One command to install.

items.summary: RTK is an open-source Rust CLI proxy that compresses shell command output before it reaches AI agent context windows, saving 60-90% tokens with under 10ms overhead.
items.reasoning: Practical tool addressing a real problem in AI coding workflows. Works with Claude Code, Cursor, Codex. Moderate novelty.

items.title: Without any subsequent fine-tuning, EMO remains robust when only a subset of experts is kept: with 2...
items.content:
Without any subsequent fine-tuning, EMO remains robust when only a subset of experts is kept: with 25% of experts, it loses ~1 percentage point in overall performance; with 12.5%, it drops ~3 points. 

Standard MoEs degrade sharply. https://t.co/CFQtno7dBh

items.title:
Imagine you check into a @Marriott hotel and one of these is walking down the hall. 

The demos are ...
items.content:
Imagine you check into a @Marriott hotel and one of these is walking down the hall. 

The demos are getting better. 

Way better.

I imagine @adcock_brett is gonna have these in at least one Marriott soon.

And if not Marriotts, I know of two "robot run" hotels coming to San Francisco before the end of the year. One is already running in Los Angeles as a test pilot to give the robot industry a place to test out robots, like these.

Welcome to the future. 

And, yeah, I know these are still years from being in my home. I'll be honest, I can't afford a $20,000 to $100,000 robot just for home use. 

But Marriott can.

Imagine you check into a @Marriott hotel and one of these is walking down the hall.

The demos are getting better.

Way better.

I imagine @adcock_brett is gonna have these in at least one Marriott soon.

And if not Marriotts, I know of two "robot run" hotels coming to San Francisco before the end of the year. One is already running in Los Angeles as a test pilot to give the robot industry a place to test out robots, like these.

Welcome to the future.

And, yeah, I know these are still years from being in my home. I'll be honest, I can't afford a $20,000 to $100,000 robot just for home use.

But Marriott can.

items.title: One of the most frustrating paradoxes in deep learning: making a model do less math often makes it r...
items.content: One of the most frustrating paradoxes in deep learning: making a model do less math often makes it run slower. Why? Because unstructured sparsity introduces irregular memory access, and GPUs are built for predictable, dense blocks of math.
items.author: @hardmaru.bsky.social
items.summary: Hardmaru explains the sparsity paradox: making a model do less math often makes it slower because unstructured sparsity creates irregular memory access patterns that GPUs can't handle efficiently.
items.reasoning: Clear explanation of a fundamental problem in ML efficiency that motivates their research.
items.summary_html: Hardmaru explains the sparsity paradox: making a model do less math often makes it slower because unstructured sparsity creates irregular memory access patterns that GPUs can't handle efficiently.
items.content_html: One of the most frustrating paradoxes in deep learning: making a model do less math often makes it run slower. Why? Because unstructured sparsity introduces irregular memory access, and GPUs are built for predictable, dense blocks of math.

items.title: The human brain is incredibly efficient because it only activates the specific neurons needed for a ...
items.content: The human brain is incredibly efficient because it only activates the specific neurons needed for a thought. Modern LLMs naturally try to do this too (over 95% of neurons in feedforward layers stay silent for any given word), but our hardware punishes them for it.
items.author: @hardmaru.bsky.social
items.summary: Hardmaru draws analogy between brain efficiency (activating only needed neurons) and LLM feedforward layers (95%+ neurons silent for any given token), noting hardware punishes this natural sparsity.
items.reasoning: Compelling framing of the research motivation connecting neuroscience inspiration with practical ML efficiency challenges.
items.summary_html: Hardmaru draws analogy between brain efficiency (activating only needed neurons) and LLM feedforward layers (95%+ neurons silent for any given token), noting hardware punishes this natural sparsity.
items.content_html: The human brain is incredibly efficient because it only activates the specific neurons needed for a thought. Modern LLMs naturally try to do this too (over 95% of neurons in feedforward layers stay silent for any given word), but our hardware punishes them for it.

items.title: Most LLMs are trained and deployed as one monolithic system, even when an application only needs a n...
items.content:
Most LLMs are trained and deployed as one monolithic system, even when an application only needs a narrow capability like code or math.

MoEs seem to break this pattern by using only a few experts per token. But across a full task, standard MoEs still rely on many experts.
items.author: @allen_ai
items.summary: Problem statement: standard MoEs still rely on many experts across full tasks despite per-token sparsity
items.reasoning: Good framing of the research problem EMO addresses

Most LLMs are trained and deployed as one monolithic system, even when an application only needs a narrow capability like code or math.

items.content:
@dileeplearning i asked about the money invested, and only some of this (if any) is relevant.  rather, most of it is an assessment of utility, which is not what I asked for. 

that said, with respect to utility, yes there is (some) utility (eg for programming) the question is whether the investments make sense economically. 

so far most of the major players aside from chip companies are spending far more than they are earning, which is not sustainable. 

the bet is about those economics and whether they will change. although you have some interesting things to say, i don’t think overall that they were responsive to what i asked.

items.title:
I'm real excited to see the new @digg that @kevinrose and team are building. 

His teases show it do...
items.content:
I'm real excited to see the new @digg that @kevinrose and team are building. 

His teases show it does a ton of stuff I haven't been able to do on https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ (and promises to do AI news from X too).

The thing is someone else already copied my site with their agents and then got me to help them. 

More on that later in May. (The company who did it gave me the site and is having me work with them to make their agents better).

There is no moat. 

There is only "better" at the moment and distribution. 

And that can go away quick. 

Competition in this new world is crazy. 

I have two sets of agents working with me and competing to make a better news site. 

And if Digg wins?

Well then my agents will have a long hard talk. :-)

By the way, it's very interesting to have two separate companies with two separate sets of agents competing. They each have a very different "taste" and way they work. 

I highly recommend doing the same for your project. 

I'm learning more faster by doing that.

Every AI video creator has this nightmare: You generate a character with one prompt… but in the next shot, she’s a different person. Different face. Different skin. Different eyes. 

You can’t tell a story like that.

items.title: When I started my PhD 20 years ago, most conferences required that I give up my copyright on the pap...
items.content:
When I started my PhD 20 years ago, most conferences required that I give up my copyright on the paper if I wanted to get published in the conference proceedings.

Conferences themselves often didn't get the copyright either. The copyright went to greedy commercial publishers like Springer and Elsevier, along with non-profit professional societies like IEEE and ACM that behaved similarly.

These bastards added little value relative to what they charged. They just took papers the production of which were paid by the public money, put them online behind a paywall, and charged $30-$50 per download.

So, if you are a scientist working in a public university, you would have to pay $30-$50 to those monopolies to download a paper of your colleague from another public university while the latter would pay the same amount to download yours.

Of course, doing research like that would become extremely expensive, so these monopolies signed unlimited download agreements with each university in the world (there are tens of thousands of them) for something like $1M/year per publisher. So, public money was paid again, now to let scientists read one another's works.

Luckily, computer science people realized with time that the public is being robbed. Several prominent researchers stood up against this situation and said that they will no longer publish their works in conferences demanding giving up the copyright.

The most respected AI conferences like NIPS, ICML, and ICLR now only license the right to publish the paper, while the authors retain the copyright.

AI journals like JMLR and JAIR went further, and all papers published in these journals are licensed under the CC-BY Creative Commons license, which allows unrestricted use by anyone for anything.

But greedy bastards still keep the copyright on research that happened before the 2020s, and thus, the universities still pay them millions every year.

Today, anyone can publish their work on arXiv, and many scientists think that this makes their work accessible to anyone and allows their research to freely spread across the globe, but this is not exactly the case.

The default arXiv license is that arXiv has a non-exclusive right to host the paper. If the scientists haven't changed the license setting when submitting their paper to arXiv, this means that only arXiv has the right to host this paper or its part. No one else, not even a scientific news outlet, can use even a small fragment from the paper in their publication.

This significantly limits the reach of such a paper. If magazines or scientific news websites cannot use the paper, then only those who know about it will read it.

If you are a young or not so young scientist, please do consider setting the CC-BY, the CC-BY-SA, or the CC-ZERO license (like this paper https://t.co/gZkUfMpQGl) when submitting to arXiv. Otherwise, only if you are from a big lab, published in a top-tier conference, or have a large network, will your paper become known and hopefully used to advance science.

items.summary: Burkov provides a detailed history of academic publishing monopolies and argues scientists should use open licenses (CC-BY, CC-BY-SA, CC-ZERO) when submitting to arXiv to maximize paper reach.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful advocacy for open science, well-written. Not AI-specific but relevant to AI research community. Moderate engagement.

Most of my non-AI sites (so everything except Photo AI, Interior AI, Ideas AI) have 99.99% profit margins now, that means very very low costs

The only high costs I have left is Photo AI which is about $20,000/month for running image models and Interior AI which is about $1,000/month!

items.summary_html: Levelsio details that most non-AI sites have 99.99% margins, while Photo AI costs $20K/mo and Interior AI $1K/mo for GPU inference
items.content_html:
Most of my non-AI sites (so everything except Photo AI, Interior AI, Ideas AI) have 99.99% profit margins now, that means very very low costs

The only high costs I have left is Photo AI which is about $20,000/month for running image models and Interior AI which is about $1,000/month!

items.content:
Grok says:

 "If you’re building anything AI-related—agents, RAG systems, personal knowledge tools, or enterprise data platforms—this hour-long demo is required viewing."

Do you listen to Grok? It is my first viewer. 

Try it on any video uploaded to X, including your own. Click the Grok button on any video post. Then ask it "please write a lengthy report on what you learned by watching this video."

Its full report is here: https://t.co/F91021v6i4

items.reasoning: Insider timeline estimate on general-purpose robotics from someone meeting with data collection teams
items.summary_html: Scobleizer estimates 3-5 years before robots can do everything desired, based on conversations with people collecting training data
items.content_html:
@TheWebBrokers1 @Marriott @adcock_brett Yeah, but how long do you think before it does literally everything we want it to do ?

I am meeting with the people collecting the data to train these robots.

I say three years. They say maybe five.

items.title:
@stefrich45040 @Marriott @adcock_brett I bet @AirBnb owners buy up every single one produced. 

Why?...
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer predicts AirBnb owners will buy every robot produced, discusses timeline of 3-5 years for mass robot production, and acknowledges need for government programs for displaced workers
items.reasoning: Combines robotics market prediction with social policy implications, thoughtful take on displacement
items.summary_html: Scobleizer predicts AirBnb owners will buy every robot produced, discusses timeline of 3-5 years for mass robot production, and acknowledges need for government programs for displaced workers
items.content_html:
@stefrich45040 @Marriott @adcock_brett I bet @AirBnb owners buy up every single one produced.

Why? They don't have hotel staff mostly. Mostly owners of properties doing this work. And who wants to make beds in the morning? No one, even the workers. It's a job to make money.

Which does bring problems for people, I understand. When robots are made by the millions (probably three years away, maybe five) we'll need new government programs to take care of people who need to put food on the table and pay their rent/mortgages.

items.title: @TheWebBrokers1 @Marriott @adcock_brett I'm very bullish on Tesla. Even if it is late, I already tru...
items.content:
@TheWebBrokers1 @Marriott @adcock_brett I'm very bullish on Tesla. Even if it is late, I already trust my life to its robots (and that of my family). 

It could come in late, after everyone ships, and still sell the majority. 

Why? 

Trust.

Oh, and by 2028 Tesla will have the Robotaxi network up and running in most places in America, at least (and probably most "richer" cities). 

What really matters? Distribution. 

Optimus and Robotaxi go together like peanut butter and jelly.

"Hey Grok, I need to go to the airport, can you send me a robotaxi along with an Optimus to help with our heavy luggage?"

items.title:
So San Francisco. 

Multiple companies having robot fights and dances. 

Great networking. The robot...
items.content:
So San Francisco. 

Multiple companies having robot fights and dances. 

Great networking. The robots don't care if we talk. :-) 

I can't make this one, already booked at Llama Lounge in Palo Alto.

Speaking of which, how does anyone get anything done in San Francisco? There are always like three events going every evening that sound interesting. 

I guess this is what's gonna happen when robots take all our jobs. 

We'll just go to events.

Now if we can figure out how to get the money to pay for them without having a job. :-)

items.title:
Better displays coming to headsets.

10,000 nits? What does that mean?

Much more colorful displays ...
items.content:
Better displays coming to headsets.

10,000 nits? What does that mean?

Much more colorful displays with much bigger dynamic range.

IE, your movie looks a lot more like you are in the Sphere in Las Vegas than watching on your dad's old color TV.

I didn't go to display week this year, been the past few years, so appreciate @SadlyItsBradley's report.

More nits is hugely important in waveguides which will soon be in AI-driven glasses.

The headsets are still too heavy and too much like a scuba mask, but at least we'll soon be rewarded for putting them on even beyond the displays in the Apple Vision Pro, which are pretty good.

items.title:
Most AI initiatives don’t fail because the models are weak.

They fail because teams give agents too...
items.content:
Most AI initiatives don’t fail because the models are weak.

They fail because teams give agents too much power, or not enough.

That’s the real scaling problem nobody talks about, and it’s exactly what I discussed with Arno van de Velde, Principal Solutions Architect at @elastic.

Here’s why modular AI, built like building blocks, changes the game. Let’s dig in.

#ElasticPartner #Elastic

items.title:
Most underrated skill today: Observability.

I feel you can build a career just on this and have 2 d...
items.content:
Most underrated skill today: Observability.

I feel you can build a career just on this and have 2 decades of guaranteed money if you learn the skill well, and I'll tell you how below.

Reality: Most teams I help look at me with dead eyes when I ask them about observability.

That's sad, but it's the current state of the industry:

• No tracing
• No structured data
• No way to understand what's happening when things break

Outside of the most sophisticated companies, that's still where most teams are.

AI is now writing a ton of code, and people are shipping this code to production.

However important observability was before, it is 10x more important today.

Believe me, you don't want to be the one pushing slop into production and then guessing how it works, with no way to understand what happens.

Here is your first step:

Honeycomb is running a FREE, 3-day virtual event focused entirely on observability.

The event is running May 12-14. Did I mention it's completely free?

Everything will revolve around observability for the agent era.

Register here: https://t.co/XbDjt6Qnm1

Thanks to the @honeycombio team for partnering with me on this post.

items.title:
@WillyB_303 Customers aren't always right. 

And I'm not just a customer, but someone with enough re...
items.content:
@WillyB_303 Customers aren't always right. 

And I'm not just a customer, but someone with enough reach and engagement to change belief about a company. 

Journalism is all about access now. What he was signaling to me is "if you aren't nice to us, you won't get invited to see the latest anymore." 

At least that's the message I heard. 

And, yeah, I agree it wasn't nice or fair. 

But Steve Jobs did the same. 

I have to decide whether I will play the game. It's not always a nice game.

@WillyB_303 Customers aren't always right.

And I'm not just a customer, but someone with enough reach and engagement to change belief about a company.

Journalism is all about access now. What he was signaling to me is "if you aren't nice to us, you won't get invited to see the latest anymore."

At least that's the message I heard.

And, yeah, I agree it wasn't nice or fair.

But Steve Jobs did the same.

I have to decide whether I will play the game. It's not always a nice game.

items.title:
The biggest “aha” from this conversation with Arno at Elastic:

AI should not be one giant brain.

→...
items.content:
The biggest “aha” from this conversation with Arno at Elastic:

AI should not be one giant brain.

→ It should be a system of small, well-defined functions
→ Each function has a clear role
→ Each agent only gets the power it needs, nothing more

This is how you avoid chaos as AI adoption grows.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon shares insight from Elastic conversation: AI should be a system of small, well-defined functions rather than one giant brain, with each agent getting only necessary power.
items.reasoning: Advocates for principle of least privilege in AI systems - important architectural concept. Elastic sponsored but contains valid design philosophy.

items.title:
Why the building block approach actually scales in the real world:

→ You can compose agents instead...
items.content:
Why the building block approach actually scales in the real world:

→ You can compose agents instead of rebuilding them
→ You control behavior by design, not by prompt hacking
→ You can change one block without breaking the whole system

This is how you go from experiments to production AI.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon explains the building block approach to AI agents: composable, controllable by design, modular changes without breaking systems.
items.reasoning: Discusses important production AI architecture principles - modularity, composability, controlled behavior. Part of Elastic sponsored content.
items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon explains the building block approach to AI agents: composable, controllable by design, modular changes without breaking systems.
items.content_html:
Why the building block approach actually scales in the real world:

→ You can compose agents instead of rebuilding them

→ You control behavior by design, not by prompt hacking

→ You can change one block without breaking the whole system

This is how you go from experiments to production AI.

items.title:
I think it's due to video resolution

When Jim Carrey got famous in the 1990s, the average TV was 72...
items.content:
I think it's due to video resolution

When Jim Carrey got famous in the 1990s, the average TV was 720×480 (480i)

Now 4K is the standard, which is 3840×2160

If you exaggerate your facial features on a blurry small low resolution TV, you probably get emotions across much better

items.title:
The trackpad of the Framework 13 Pro is one of its highlights.

I'm running Omarchy, and there was s...
items.content:
The trackpad of the Framework 13 Pro is one of its highlights.

I'm running Omarchy, and there was something that wasn't working quite well for me (coming from a Mac) with the trackpad.

I finally realized, I couldn't select text (click and drag with the trackpad).

3 lines of configuration later, and the problem is now gone.

This is one of the best trackpads I've tried. I can't really find any differences with my Mac trackpad.

Among hundreds of examples. Wrote eight books about the future. And have built the most complete lists of the AI industry here on X: https://t.co/9eRY65x3IQ including 8,500 companies. And my photography has been published many places.

Or maybe it's just cause I have 570,000 followers. :-)

Whatever the reason, it's greatly appreciated that I can put food on the table with my work.

items.title: I saw a UAP in my neighborhood. This Unidentified Ambulating Person just walked right past my house ...
items.content:
I saw a UAP in my neighborhood. This Unidentified Ambulating Person just walked right past my house without identifying himself.

I also saw a UFO fly directly over my neighborhood. I could not tell if it was a United Airlines flight or a Delta Airlines flight, but it probably was a FedEx flight. I just couldn’t identify it definitively.

UAPs and UFOs are real — “unidentified”!
